stage.name: What CVA6 is not: IP, core, cache, and SoC boundaries
stage.description: A source-grounded OpenHW SlideKit microcourse that uses the official CVA6 scope diagram to separate core IP responsibilities from surrounding SoC integration.
stage.courseTemplateName: Professional Technical Deep Dive - Evidence-Grounded SlideKit

scenes.title: What CVA6 is not: boundaries before modules

content.title: What CVA6 is not: boundaries before modules
content.subtitle: A practical lesson on IP, core, cache, interface, and SoC boundaries.
content.thesis: A good CVA6 reader does not ask only what is inside the core. They also ask what is deliberately outside the IP boundary.

sourceAnchors.title: Scope of the CVA6 IP
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The documented IP includes the CVA6 core, L1 write-through cache, optional FPU/MMU/PMP, CSR, performance counters, AXI interface, P-Mesh interface, and CV-X-IF, while several system-level blocks are outside the IP.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 requirements scope of the IP
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The requirements specification defines the IP scope as the CVA6 hardware supporting product features, breaks verification scope into DUTs, and excludes several system-level or software concerns.

content.speakerNotes: Lesson one defined CVA6. Lesson two protects that definition from becoming too broad. CVA6 is a processor core IP, but the word IP does not mean it contains every interrupt controller, debug block, cache style, board boot flow, or verification environment. Today we use the official scope diagram to draw boundaries. That may sound less exciting than pipeline magic, but it is what keeps later technical claims honest.

actions.text: Lesson one defined CVA6. Lesson two protects that definition from becoming too broad. CVA6 is a processor core IP, but the word IP does not mean it contains every interrupt controller, debug block, cache style, board boot flow, or verification environment. Today we use the official scope diagram to draw boundaries. That may sound less exciting than pipeline magic, but it is what keeps later technical claims honest.

content.title: Why boundary reading matters
content.subtitle: The same diagram answers three different engineering questions.

cards.label: Learning
cards.title: Do not mix layers
cards.body: Core behavior, interfaces, system blocks, and verification targets should be learned with different questions.

cards.label: Integration
cards.title: Interfaces are not the SoC
cards.body: AXI, P-Mesh, and CV-X-IF make integration possible, but they do not magically include the surrounding system.

cards.label: Verification
cards.title: Scope becomes DUT scope
cards.body: The requirements document links IP scope to verification scope, so boundary mistakes become test-planning mistakes.

sourceAnchors.title: User Manual target audience
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The user manual targets software programmers, hardware integrators, coprocessor architects, implementation engineers, and verification engineers rather than only internal RTL designers.

sourceAnchors.title: Scope of the CVA6 IP
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The documented IP includes the CVA6 core, L1 write-through cache, optional FPU/MMU/PMP, CSR, performance counters, AXI interface, P-Mesh interface, and CV-X-IF, while several system-level blocks are outside the IP.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 requirements scope of the IP
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The requirements specification defines the IP scope as the CVA6 hardware supporting product features, breaks verification scope into DUTs, and excludes several system-level or software concerns.

content.speakerNotes: Boundary reading matters because different readers ask different questions. A software programmer may care about what the core exposes. A hardware integrator may care about AXI or interrupt wiring. A verification engineer asks what belongs in the DUT and what belongs in the surrounding environment. The official manual is not just a pile of text. It is telling us not to flatten core, interface, system, and verification concerns into one blob.

actions.text: Boundary reading matters because different readers ask different questions. A software programmer may care about what the core exposes. A hardware integrator may care about AXI or interrupt wiring. A verification engineer asks what belongs in the DUT and what belongs in the surrounding environment. The official manual is not just a pile of text. It is telling us not to flatten core, interface, system, and verification concerns into one blob.

scenes.title: Read the scope diagram as a contract

content.title: Read the scope diagram as a contract
content.body: The scope diagram is not decorative. It defines what this documentation is willing to call CVA6 IP.
content.caption: CVA6 scope diagram: core, L1 write-through cache, optional units, CSR/counters, and interfaces inside the IP boundary.

callouts.title: Inside boundary
callouts.body: CVA6 core, L1 write-through cache, optional FPU/MMU/PMP, CSR, counters, AXI, P-Mesh, and CV-X-IF.

callouts.title: Outside boundary
callouts.body: CLINT, PLIC, debug module, and L1 write-back cache support are explicitly outside the documented IP.

callouts.title: Reading rule
callouts.body: If a topic crosses the boundary, name it as integration, not as pure core microarchitecture.

assets.caption: Official CVA6 scope diagram used to separate IP internals from surrounding system blocks.

sourceAnchors.title: Scope of the CVA6 IP
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The documented IP includes the CVA6 core, L1 write-through cache, optional FPU/MMU/PMP, CSR, performance counters, AXI interface, P-Mesh interface, and CV-X-IF, while several system-level blocks are outside the IP.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 requirements scope of the IP
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The requirements specification defines the IP scope as the CVA6 hardware supporting product features, breaks verification scope into DUTs, and excludes several system-level or software concerns.

content.speakerNotes: This is the key slide. Read the scope diagram as a contract. Inside the boundary are the CVA6 core, L1 write-through cache, optional FPU, MMU, PMP, CSR, performance counters, and interfaces such as AXI, P-Mesh, and CV-X-IF. Outside are CLINT, PLIC, debug module, and L1 write-back cache support. The important habit is simple: when a topic crosses the boundary, call it integration instead of pretending it is all core internals.

actions.text: This is the key slide. Read the scope diagram as a contract. Inside the boundary are the CVA6 core, L1 write-through cache, optional FPU, MMU, PMP, CSR, performance counters, and interfaces such as AXI, P-Mesh, and CV-X-IF. Outside are CLINT, PLIC, debug module, and L1 write-back cache support. The important habit is simple: when a topic crosses the boundary, call it integration instead of pretending it is all core internals.

scenes.title: What is inside the CVA6 IP boundary

content.title: What is inside the CVA6 IP boundary
content.subtitle: Inside does not mean equally central. It means the documented IP owns the responsibility.

items.title: Core and pipeline
items.body: The CVA6 core itself is the main study object for later pipeline, issue, execute, and commit lessons.

items.title: Optional units
items.body: FPU, MMU, and PMP may be present depending on configuration, so they are inside the family but not always active.

items.title: CSR and counters
items.body: CSR and performance counters connect hardware behavior to software-visible state and requirements.

items.title: Interfaces
items.body: AXI, P-Mesh, and CV-X-IF are IP boundary interfaces. They are doors, not the whole building.

content.takeaway: Inside the IP means CVA6 has responsibility for the contract, but configuration still decides which details are active.

sourceAnchors.title: Scope of the CVA6 IP
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The documented IP includes the CVA6 core, L1 write-through cache, optional FPU/MMU/PMP, CSR, performance counters, AXI interface, P-Mesh interface, and CV-X-IF, while several system-level blocks are outside the IP.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 requirements scope of the IP
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The requirements specification defines the IP scope as the CVA6 hardware supporting product features, breaks verification scope into DUTs, and excludes several system-level or software concerns.

content.speakerNotes: Inside the IP boundary, not every item has the same teaching role. The core and pipeline are the central study object. Optional FPU, MMU, and PMP tell us that configuration matters. CSR and counters connect hardware to software-visible behavior. Interfaces such as AXI, P-Mesh, and CV-X-IF are boundary contracts. I like the door analogy here: an interface is a door in the building, not the entire city outside the building.

actions.text: Inside the IP boundary, not every item has the same teaching role. The core and pipeline are the central study object. Optional FPU, MMU, and PMP tell us that configuration matters. CSR and counters connect hardware to software-visible behavior. Interfaces such as AXI, P-Mesh, and CV-X-IF are boundary contracts. I like the door analogy here: an interface is a door in the building, not the entire city outside the building.

scenes.title: What is outside: system blocks and integration responsibility

content.title: What is outside: system blocks and integration responsibility
content.subtitle: Outside does not mean unimportant. It means the responsibility belongs to the system around CVA6.

columns.label: System blocks
columns.title: Interrupt and debug infrastructure
bullets: CLINT and PLIC are interrupt-related system blocks, not CVA6 core internals.
A debug module such as DTM is also outside the documented IP boundary.
CVA6 may interface with these blocks, but interfacing is not the same as owning them.
When debugging boot or interrupt behavior, decide whether the question is core-side or platform-side.

columns.label: System behavior
columns.title: Board, software, and platform flow
bullets: Boot flow, firmware image, board memory map, and OS integration are system topics.
The requirements document excludes software emulation of optional RISC-V extensions from IP scope.
SoC-level verification may wrap CVA6, but the wrapper is not automatically CVA6 itself.
Good notes should label the layer: core, interface, subsystem, platform, or verification harness.

anchor.label: Boundary sentence
anchor.text: Outside the IP can still be essential, but it should be named as integration or platform responsibility.

sourceAnchors.title: Scope of the CVA6 IP
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The documented IP includes the CVA6 core, L1 write-through cache, optional FPU/MMU/PMP, CSR, performance counters, AXI interface, P-Mesh interface, and CV-X-IF, while several system-level blocks are outside the IP.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 requirements out-of-scope list
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The requirements specification excludes system-level blocks such as CLINT/PLIC/debug module, L1 write-back cache support, and also excludes software emulation of optional RISC-V extensions from the IP scope.

content.speakerNotes: Outside the boundary does not mean irrelevant. Interrupt controllers, debug modules, firmware, board memory maps, and operating-system boot flows are essential in real systems. But they are not automatically CVA6 internals. If a bug appears during boot or interrupt handling, a careful engineer asks which layer owns the symptom. Is it core behavior, an interface contract, the surrounding subsystem, platform software, or the verification harness? That one question prevents many long detours.

actions.text: Outside the boundary does not mean irrelevant. Interrupt controllers, debug modules, firmware, board memory maps, and operating-system boot flows are essential in real systems. But they are not automatically CVA6 internals. If a bug appears during boot or interrupt handling, a careful engineer asks which layer owns the symptom. Is it core behavior, an interface contract, the surrounding subsystem, platform software, or the verification harness? That one question prevents many long detours.

scenes.title: Cache boundary: write-through is inside, write-back support is outside

content.title: Cache boundary: write-through is inside, write-back support is outside
content.subtitle: Cache wording is where boundary mistakes often start.

columns.label: Inside
columns.title: L1 write-through cache
bullets: The scope list includes L1 write-through cache as part of the IP.
The requirements define L1 write-through data cache and L1 instruction cache behavior.
Cache size and organization can still be configuration-dependent.
This is a legitimate CVA6 learning topic, but it belongs in the memory-system unit later.

columns.label: Outside
columns.title: L1 write-back cache support
bullets: The manual explicitly says L1 write-back cache support is not part of the IP.
Do not use generic cache knowledge to claim CVA6 owns a write-back cache path.
If write-back support appears in a system, treat it as surrounding integration unless a source says otherwise.
This is exactly why source anchors matter in technical teaching.

anchor.label: Careful claim
anchor.text: CVA6 includes L1 write-through cache in the documented IP scope; L1 write-back cache support is outside that scope.

sourceAnchors.title: Scope of the CVA6 IP
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The documented IP includes the CVA6 core, L1 write-through cache, optional FPU/MMU/PMP, CSR, performance counters, AXI interface, P-Mesh interface, and CV-X-IF, while several system-level blocks are outside the IP.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 L1 cache requirements boundary
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The requirements distinguish L1 write-through data cache and L1 instruction cache as part of CVA6, while L1 write-back cache support is outside the documented IP scope.

content.speakerNotes: Cache is a good stress test for careful wording. The scope includes L1 write-through cache. The requirements document has separate requirements for the L1 write-through data cache and L1 instruction cache. But L1 write-back cache support is explicitly outside the documented IP scope. So a professional classroom should not say CVA6 just has whatever cache story we remember from computer architecture class. It should say exactly what the sources support.

actions.text: Cache is a good stress test for careful wording. The scope includes L1 write-through cache. The requirements document has separate requirements for the L1 write-through data cache and L1 instruction cache. But L1 write-back cache support is explicitly outside the documented IP scope. So a professional classroom should not say CVA6 just has whatever cache story we remember from computer architecture class. It should say exactly what the sources support.

content.title: A five-question boundary checklist
content.subtitle: Use this before turning a CVA6 topic into a slide, issue, or verification task.

steps.title: Layer
steps.body: Is the question about core, interface, subsystem, platform, or verification harness?

steps.title: Source
steps.body: Which official page says this item is inside or outside the IP?

steps.title: Config
steps.body: Is the item optional or configuration-dependent?

steps.title: Owner
steps.body: Who owns the fix or explanation: core team, integrator, software, or DV?

steps.title: Claim
steps.body: Can the final statement avoid over-claiming beyond the source?

content.takeaway: This checklist is small, but it is the difference between a clean architecture lesson and a pile of adjacent facts.

sourceAnchors.title: Scope of the CVA6 IP
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The documented IP includes the CVA6 core, L1 write-through cache, optional FPU/MMU/PMP, CSR, performance counters, AXI interface, P-Mesh interface, and CV-X-IF, while several system-level blocks are outside the IP.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 requirements scope of the IP
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The requirements specification defines the IP scope as the CVA6 hardware supporting product features, breaks verification scope into DUTs, and excludes several system-level or software concerns.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 requirements out-of-scope list
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The requirements specification excludes system-level blocks such as CLINT/PLIC/debug module, L1 write-back cache support, and also excludes software emulation of optional RISC-V extensions from the IP scope.

content.speakerNotes: Here is the working checklist. First, name the layer. Second, point to the source that places the item inside or outside the IP. Third, ask whether configuration changes the answer. Fourth, identify the owner: core, integrator, software, or verification. Fifth, write the claim without overreaching. This may look simple, but it is the difference between a clean engineering lesson and a pile of adjacent facts wearing a trench coat.

actions.text: Here is the working checklist. First, name the layer. Second, point to the source that places the item inside or outside the IP. Third, ask whether configuration changes the answer. Fourth, identify the owner: core, integrator, software, or verification. Fifth, write the claim without overreaching. This may look simple, but it is the difference between a clean engineering lesson and a pile of adjacent facts wearing a trench coat.

scenes.title: Checkpoint: can you place the boundary?

questions.question: Which item is explicitly listed outside the documented CVA6 IP scope?
questions.analysis: The scope list places optional MMU, CSR/counters, and AXI inside the IP boundary, while CLINT or PLIC interrupt modules are explicitly outside.

questions.question: Which statements are careful, source-supported CVA6 boundary claims?

options.label: CVA6 includes the core and L1 write-through cache in the documented IP scope
CVA6 owns every board-level boot-flow and firmware decision
AXI and CV-X-IF are interfaces that help connect CVA6 to a system
L1 write-back cache support is outside the documented IP scope

questions.analysis: A, C, and D follow the official scope and requirements text. B over-claims system-level responsibilities as CVA6 internals.

questions.question: A boot failure appears when CVA6 is integrated into a larger platform. What is the best first engineering question?

options.label: Which layer owns the symptom: core, interface, subsystem, platform software, or verification harness?
Can we assume the bug is inside the CVA6 pipeline because CVA6 is the CPU?
Should we ignore the scope diagram and debug every repository at once?
Should we treat CLINT, PLIC, and debug module as CVA6 internal modules?

questions.analysis: The lesson goal is boundary-first debugging. A boot symptom may cross layers, so the first move is to locate ownership before blaming core internals.

questions.question: Why does this lesson distinguish L1 write-through cache from L1 write-back cache support?

options.label: Because the official scope includes L1 write-through cache but excludes L1 write-back cache support
Because write-through and write-back are identical in every processor
Because cache topics are never part of CVA6 learning
Because the scope diagram says all possible cache systems belong inside CVA6

questions.analysis: This distinction is directly grounded in the scope and requirements material. It is a small wording detail with a large effect on technical accuracy.

actions.text: Now let us check the boundary habit. These questions are not about memorizing every acronym. They ask whether you can place a topic in the right layer. If you can separate IP internals, boundary interfaces, outside system blocks, and cache nuance, you are ready to read later architecture lessons with much less confusion.

scenes.title: Summary: boundaries make later depth possible

content.title: Boundaries make later depth possible
takeaways: CVA6 should be read as a processor core IP, not a complete SoC.
Inside the documented IP: core, L1 write-through cache, optional units, CSR/counters, and boundary interfaces.
Outside the documented IP: CLINT/PLIC, debug module, L1 write-back cache support, and broader platform flow.
A good CVA6 claim names the layer, cites the source, and checks configuration before going deeper.
content.nextStep: Next: README, User Manual, and Design Docs - which source should answer which question?
content.closing: If lesson one said define CVA6, lesson two says do not let the definition leak into the whole system.

sourceAnchors.title: Scope of the CVA6 IP
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The documented IP includes the CVA6 core, L1 write-through cache, optional FPU/MMU/PMP, CSR, performance counters, AXI interface, P-Mesh interface, and CV-X-IF, while several system-level blocks are outside the IP.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 requirements scope of the IP
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The requirements specification defines the IP scope as the CVA6 hardware supporting product features, breaks verification scope into DUTs, and excludes several system-level or software concerns.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 requirements out-of-scope list
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The requirements specification excludes system-level blocks such as CLINT/PLIC/debug module, L1 write-back cache support, and also excludes software emulation of optional RISC-V extensions from the IP scope.

content.speakerNotes: Let us close. CVA6 is a processor core IP, not a complete SoC. Inside the documented boundary we have the core, L1 write-through cache, optional units, CSR, counters, and interfaces. Outside are CLINT, PLIC, debug module, L1 write-back cache support, and broader platform flow. The next lesson will shift from boundary to source literacy: README, User Manual, and Design Docs each answer different questions, and mixing them is another easy way to get lost.

Outside are CLINT, PLIC, debug module, L1 write-back cache support, and broader platform flow. The next lesson will shift from boundary to source literacy: README, User Manual, and Design Docs each answer different questions, and mixing them is another easy way to get lost.
